$comment: Module 5 - Quantum Error Correction & Noise question bank. One item per objective, tagged by objective_id. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views. JSON backslashes single-escaped -> one literal backslash for KaTeX.
title: Module 5 - Quantum Error Correction & Noise

quizzes.description: T1/T2, Pauli and depolarizing channels, Bloch-vector shrinkage, dephasing effect on coherences.

questions.text: What is the difference between $T_1$ and $T_2$?
questions.hint: Relaxation loses energy; dephasing loses phase coherence.
questions.explanation: $T_1$ governs decay of the excited-state population (relaxation); $T_2$ governs decay of the off-diagonal coherences (dephasing), bounded by $T_2 \le 2T_1$.

options.text: $T_1$ is the energy-relaxation time ($|1\rangle\to|0\rangle$); $T_2$ is the dephasing time (loss of relative phase), with $T_2 \le 2T_1$
$T_1$ is dephasing and $T_2$ is relaxation
they are two names for the same timescale
$T_1$ and $T_2$ describe gate speed, not noise

questions.text: Which correctly identifies the bit-flip, phase-flip, and depolarizing channels?
questions.hint: Each channel is a probabilistic Pauli (or full mixing) map.
questions.explanation: Bit-flip: $\rho\mapsto(1-p)\rho+pX\rho X$; phase-flip uses $Z$; depolarizing mixes toward $I/2$ by applying a uniformly random Pauli, i.e. $\rho\mapsto(1-p)\rho+p\,I/2$.

options.text: bit-flip applies $X$ w.p. $p$; phase-flip applies $Z$ w.p. $p$; depolarizing sends the state toward $I/2$ (a random Pauli) w.p. $p$
all three apply only $X$ errors
depolarizing applies $H$ with probability $p$
phase-flip applies $X$ and bit-flip applies $Z$

questions.text: A noise channel shrinks the Bloch vector, moving a pure state on the sphere toward the maximally mixed state at the centre of the ball.
questions.hint: Mixing reduces $|\vec r|$ and hence purity.
questions.explanation: True. Noise contracts the Bloch vector (e.g. depolarizing scales $\vec r$ by $(1-p)$), so a pure state ($|\vec r|=1$) becomes mixed ($|\vec r|<1$) — coherence is lost.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: A dephasing (phase-flip) channel with error probability $p=0.25$ multiplies the off-diagonal density-matrix elements $\rho_{01}$ by what factor $(1-2p)$?
questions.hint: Dephasing scales coherences by $(1-2p)$.
questions.explanation: $1-2p = 1-2(0.25) = 0.5$: the coherences are halved. At $p=0.5$ they vanish entirely (full dephasing).

quizzes.description: Repetition encoding, syndrome from parity checks, why measure ancillas, blindness to phase errors.

questions.text: The 3-qubit bit-flip code encodes a logical qubit as:
questions.hint: Two CNOTs entangle the data across three qubits — not cloning.
questions.explanation: The code maps $|0\rangle\to|000\rangle$, $|1\rangle\to|111\rangle$ (via CNOTs, respecting no-cloning). A single bit flip moves the state to a detectable orthogonal subspace.

options.text: $\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle \mapsto \alpha|000\rangle+\beta|111\rangle$, protecting against a single $X$ error
three independent copies $(\alpha|0\rangle+\beta|1\rangle)^{\otimes 3}$ (cloning)
$\alpha|000\rangle+\beta|110\rangle$
$|000\rangle$ regardless of the input

questions.text: With parity checks $Z_1Z_2$ and $Z_2Z_3$, a single $X$ error on the middle qubit (qubit 2) gives which syndrome?
questions.hint: Qubit 2 participates in both parity checks.
questions.explanation: An $X$ on qubit 2 flips both $Z_1Z_2$ and $Z_2Z_3$, giving syndrome $(1,1)$. Qubit 1 gives $(1,0)$, qubit 3 gives $(0,1)$, no error gives $(0,0)$.

questions.text: Why is the syndrome extracted via ancilla parity measurements rather than by measuring the data qubits?
questions.hint: The checks commute with the logical operators.
questions.explanation: Measuring $Z_1Z_2$, $Z_2Z_3$ (on ancillas) projects only the error, learning parities but not the logical amplitudes, so the encoded superposition survives.

options.text: the parity checks reveal which qubits disagree without revealing $\alpha,\beta$, so the encoded superposition is not collapsed
measuring the data is faster
ancillas are cheaper to build
data measurement would give the same information anyway

questions.text: Why can't the 3-qubit bit-flip code correct a phase-flip ($Z$) error?
questions.hint: $Z$ leaves every $ZZ$ parity unchanged.
questions.explanation: A $Z$ error commutes with $Z_1Z_2$ and $Z_2Z_3$, so the syndrome stays $(0,0)$ while the logical state acquires an undetected relative phase — the code is blind to phase flips.

options.text: a $Z$ error commutes with the $Z$-parity checks and acts within the code space (changing the relative sign), so it produces no syndrome
phase flips never occur in practice
the code has too few qubits to detect any error
$Z$ errors are automatically corrected by the CNOTs

quizzes.description: Hadamard-conjugated phase-flip code, 9-qubit concatenation, error discretization, code parameters.

questions.text: How is a phase-flip code obtained from the bit-flip code?
questions.hint: $H$ maps $Z\leftrightarrow X$, so bit-flip machinery catches phase flips.
questions.explanation: Working in the $\{|+\rangle,|-\rangle\}$ basis (Hadamard-conjugated), a $Z$ error looks like a bit flip and is caught by $X_1X_2$, $X_2X_3$ parity checks.

options.text: conjugate by Hadamards: encode $|{+}{+}{+}\rangle,|{-}{-}{-}\rangle$ and check $X$-parities, turning $Z$ errors into detectable flips
add three more data qubits
measure in the computational basis twice
replace all CNOTs with SWAPs

questions.text: The 9-qubit Shor code is built how?
questions.hint: Outer phase-flip code, inner bit-flip code.
questions.explanation: Shor's code nests a bit-flip (inner) code inside a phase-flip (outer) code; the inner code catches any $X$, the outer any $Z$, and together any $Y=iXZ$ — hence any single-qubit error.

options.text: by concatenation: encode against phase flips into 3 blocks, then encode each block against bit flips
as nine independent copies of the input qubit
by a single layer of Hadamards on 9 qubits
as a phase-flip code on 9 physical qubits with no nesting

questions.text: Error discretization explains why:
questions.hint: An arbitrary error is a linear combination of Paulis; measuring collapses it.
questions.explanation: Any single-qubit error is $E=c_0 I+c_x X+c_y Y+c_z Z$; measuring the syndrome projects the state onto one Pauli branch, so correcting the finite Pauli set corrects the whole continuum.

options.text: correcting the discrete errors $\{I, X, Y, Z\}$ suffices to correct any continuous single-qubit error, because syndrome measurement projects the error onto a Pauli
errors can only ever be exactly $X$, $Y$, or $Z$ physically
continuous errors are uncorrectable
measurement creates the errors it detects

questions.text: For the Shor code with parameters $[[9,1,3]]$, what do the three numbers mean?
questions.hint: $[[n,k,d]]$: physical, logical, distance.
questions.explanation: $[[9,1,3]]$ encodes $k=1$ logical qubit into $n=9$ physical qubits with distance $d=3$, correcting up to $\lfloor(3-1)/2\rfloor=1$ arbitrary single-qubit error.

options.text: $n=9$ physical qubits, $k=1$ logical qubit, distance $d=3$ (corrects any single error)
9 logical qubits, 1 physical qubit, 3 gates
9 gates, 1 measurement, 3 ancillas
$n=9$ errors corrected, $k=1$ syndrome, $d=3$ qubits

quizzes.description: Stabilizer formalism, CSS from Hamming code, distance vs correctable errors, Steane parameters.

questions.text: A stabilizer code defines its code space as:
questions.hint: Errors that anticommute with a stabilizer flip its measured eigenvalue.
questions.explanation: The code space is fixed by $S_i|\psi\rangle=+|\psi\rangle$ for all commuting stabilizers $S_i$; an error anticommuting with some $S_i$ yields a detectable $-1$ syndrome.

options.text: the simultaneous $+1$ eigenspace of a set of commuting Pauli operators (the stabilizers)
the kernel of a single non-Hermitian operator
any 2-dimensional subspace chosen at random
the $-1$ eigenspace of the identity

questions.text: The 7-qubit Steane code is:
questions.hint: CSS uses two classical codes; Steane uses the Hamming code for both.
questions.explanation: Steane is the CSS code from the $[7,4,3]$ Hamming code used for both $X$ and $Z$ correction, giving six stabilizer generators and a highly symmetric, transversal-friendly structure.

options.text: a CSS code built from the classical $[7,4,3]$ Hamming code, with three $X$-type and three $Z$-type stabilizers
a repetition code on 7 qubits
identical to the 9-qubit Shor code
a non-stabilizer code with no syndrome

questions.text: A stabilizer code of distance $d=7$ can correct how many arbitrary single-qubit errors, $t=\lfloor(d-1)/2\rfloor$?
questions.hint: $t=\lfloor(d-1)/2\rfloor$.
questions.explanation: $t=\lfloor(7-1)/2\rfloor=\lfloor 3\rfloor=3$ errors. Distance sets the correction radius just as in classical coding.

questions.text: How do the Steane and Shor codes compare?
questions.hint: Same $k$ and $d$, fewer physical qubits for Steane.
questions.explanation: Both protect one logical qubit at distance 3, correcting any single error; Steane $[[7,1,3]]$ does so with 7 qubits versus Shor's 9, with a more symmetric CSS structure.

options.text: Steane is $[[7,1,3]]$ and Shor is $[[9,1,3]]$: both have distance 3 and one logical qubit, but Steane uses two fewer physical qubits
Steane is $[[9,1,3]]$ and Shor is $[[7,1,3]]$
both use exactly 7 physical qubits
Steane has distance 5

quizzes.quiz_title: Surface Codes & the Threshold Theorem - Assessment
quizzes.description: 2D lattice checks, threshold theorem, syndrome matching, overhead.

questions.text: The surface code stores a logical qubit using:
questions.hint: Only nearest-neighbour checks — that's the hardware appeal.
questions.explanation: Qubits sit on a 2D lattice; star operators (products of $X$) and plaquette operators (products of $Z$) are local four-body checks, and the logical qubit is stored non-locally in the lattice topology.

options.text: a 2D lattice of qubits with local four-qubit star ($X$-type) and plaquette ($Z$-type) stabilizer checks
a single long chain of qubits with global parity checks
nine qubits in a Shor-code arrangement
non-local checks spanning the whole chip at once

questions.text: The threshold theorem states that:
questions.hint: Below threshold, bigger codes win.
questions.explanation: For $p<p_{th}$, increasing distance $d$ drives the logical error rate down as $\sim(p/p_{th})^{d/2}$ — arbitrarily reliable computation from imperfect components, at the cost of more qubits.

options.text: if the physical error rate is below a threshold $p_{th}$, the logical error rate can be suppressed arbitrarily by increasing the code distance
quantum error correction is impossible for any error rate
errors vanish entirely once any code is used
the logical error rate equals the physical error rate always

questions.text: How are errors located and corrected in the surface code?
questions.hint: Interior parities cancel; only chain endpoints light up.
questions.explanation: A chain of errors flips only its endpoint stabilizers (the interior cancels mod 2); a minimum-weight matching decoder pairs the lit endpoints and applies a correcting chain between them.

options.text: an error chain flips the syndromes at its two endpoints; a matching decoder pairs flipped checks and applies a connecting correction chain
every individual erroneous qubit lights its own unique check with no ambiguity
the data qubits are measured directly to find errors
errors are found by measuring the logical qubit repeatedly

questions.text: What is the main practical cost of surface-code fault tolerance?
questions.hint: Low error rates demand large code distance, hence many qubits.
questions.explanation: Achieving algorithmically useful logical error rates needs large code distance, translating to ~$10^3$ physical qubits per logical qubit — the central scaling challenge of fault-tolerant quantum computing.

options.text: large qubit overhead — often thousands of physical qubits per logical qubit at useful error rates
it requires no extra qubits at all
it only works at zero temperature with one qubit
it eliminates the need for any classical processing
